message:
refactor(storage): split label-filter helpers into IN-form vs JOIN-form

Reorganizes the label-filter SQL emission from a single helper with an
allow_uncorrelated_in branch into three focused helpers, each with a
single output shape. The previous "one helper that emits either IN or
EXISTS depending on a flag" was a halfway shape — it conflated the
two distinct SQL strategies (uncorrelated IN, correlated EXISTS)
behind a single name and made the call site less readable.

Concretely:

== `append_label_membership_filters` (kept, narrowed) ==

Now emits ONLY the uncorrelated IN form. Drops the
`allow_uncorrelated_in` parameter since this helper is exclusively
the fast-path option. Single label collapses to a flat IN
subquery; multi-label uses IN + GROUP BY HAVING COUNT(DISTINCT) =
N as before. The function shrinks by ~50% as the EXISTS branch
relocates.

== `append_label_and_membership_joins` (new) ==

The slow-path AND filter now emits JOINs rather than correlated
EXISTS subqueries. SQLite's planner handles JOINs against the
labels(label) index more naturally than per-row EXISTS
subqueries: the join produces a row-set that's narrowed once
against the labels index, then the outer query joins
`issues.id = ...` on top. With multi-label AND, each label gets
its own JOIN with a unique alias (`labels_and_1`,
`labels_and_2`, ...) so the planner can pick the smallest-result
label first and chain the others against the survivors. Dedup
inputs via the same `HashSet<&str>` pattern the IN form uses, so
duplicate labels collapse to a single JOIN.

The reason this is a separate helper rather than a flag-driven
branch on the previous one: the JOIN form contributes to the
query's FROM/JOIN section, while the IN form contributes to the
WHERE section. They're not interchangeable inside the same SQL
clause; trying to emit both from one helper meant the helper
needed to know whether the caller had committed to a WHERE-vs-
JOIN strategy yet, which is exactly the call-site decision the
two-helper split makes explicit.

== `append_label_or_membership_exists` (new) ==

The OR filter has only one shape that makes sense — a single
EXISTS with an IN predicate on the labels table — so it gets
its own helper that always emits that form. No mode flag, no
duplicate scaffolding inside an over-general helper. Skips
emission entirely when `labels_or.is_empty()` (the legacy code
guarded this inside the umbrella helper; the standalone helper
hoists the guard to the top so callers can grep for the no-op
contract directly).

== Call-site changes ==

The ready-issues query path that previously called
`append_label_membership_filters(... allow_uncorrelated_in)` now
branches on the same boolean explicitly:

  - When the boolean is true, append IN-form via
    `append_label_membership_filters` (no other predicates yet,
    so IN is the right plan).
  - When false, the call site:
      1. Appends the AND-form JOINs via
         `append_label_and_membership_joins` BEFORE the WHERE
         clause (since they belong in FROM/JOIN, not WHERE).
      2. Then writes ` WHERE 1=1` — the JOINs already narrowed.
      3. Then appends the OR-form EXISTS predicate via
         `append_label_or_membership_exists` AFTER the WHERE.

The order matters. Trying to write `WHERE 1=1` first and then
splicing JOINs into the FROM clause after-the-fact would require
either an SQL string rewrite or a more complex builder; the
two-helper split lets the call site assemble the parts in their
natural order.

Same SQL semantics as before — both shapes produce identical row
sets — but the planner now gets a strictly better plan for the
slow path (JOINs against the labels index instead of correlated
EXISTS subqueries), and the call site reads as "explicitly
branch on which strategy applies" rather than "pass a flag and
hope the inner helper picks correctly."
